IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important practical problem of enforcing lexical constraints in black-box sequence models and introduces Grid Beam Search (GBS), a model-agnostic decoder that handles multi-token and discontinuous constraints and enables subword handling for unseen tokens — features that can materially change how practitioners perform interactive post-editing and domain adaptation (e.g., reported BLEU gains on multiple corpora and Pick-Revise scenarios). From a theory-focused perspective the contribution is strong because it provides a broadly applicable algorithmic extension to beam search, but it stops short of formal guarantees about correctness, completeness of constraint satisfaction, or tight worst-case complexity bounds beyond the stated O(ktc) cost and parallelization arguments; these gaps mean the work is high-impact and likely to be widely used, but not yet transformative in a formal/algorithmic sense. | B(4): This paper tackles an important, practical problem—allowing user- or domain-specified lexical constraints during sequence decoding—using a model-agnostic method (Grid Beam Search) that requires no retraining and thus is easy to integrate into production pipelines. The demonstrated gains in interactive post-editing and domain adaptation scenarios, plus support for multi-token/discontinuous constraints and subword handling for rare tokens, make it likely practitioners will adopt or build on the approach. The main barrier is the increased decoding cost (O(ktc)), though the authors note parallelizability to mitigate this; overall the work is likely to have strong applied impact without being wholly transformative to core modeling paradigms.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important practical problem of enforcing lexical constraints in black-box sequence models and introduces Grid Beam Search (GBS), a model-agnostic decoder that handles multi-token and discontinuous constraints and enables subword handling for unseen tokens — features that can materially change how practitioners perform interactive post-editing and domain adaptation (e.g., reported BLEU gains on multiple corpora and Pick-Revise scenarios). From a theory-focused perspective the contribution is strong because it provides a broadly applicable algorithmic extension to beam search, but it stops short of formal guarantees about correctness, completeness of constraint satisfaction, or tight worst-case complexity bounds beyond the stated O(ktc) cost and parallelization arguments; these gaps mean the work is high-impact and likely to be widely used, but not yet transformative in a formal/algorithmic sense.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—allowing user- or domain-specified lexical constraints during sequence decoding—using a model-agnostic method (Grid Beam Search) that requires no retraining and thus is easy to integrate into production pipelines. The demonstrated gains in interactive post-editing and domain adaptation scenarios, plus support for multi-token/discontinuous constraints and subword handling for rare tokens, make it likely practitioners will adopt or build on the approach. The main barrier is the increased decoding cost (O(ktc)), though the authors note parallelizability to mitigate this; overall the work is likely to have strong applied impact without being wholly transformative to core modeling paradigms.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and flows logically from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are clearly stated early (GBS, coverage vectors/filters, subword handling). Notation appears consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, with thorough related work and reported hyperparameters; figures/tables/examples are effective in illustrating GBS. A few presentation gaps remain: the description of coverage vectors/filters and the runtime complexity claim would benefit from a slightly more formal specification and clearer illustrations of the grid-beam mechanics for the reader. Overall the exposition is strong with only minor clarity improvements needed. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and states its main contributions early (GBS description, support for multi-token/discontinuous constraints, subword handling). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, and experimental details (datasets, baselines, hyperparameters) are provided, which aids reproducibility. The summary does not explicitly confirm clarity of notation or the effectiveness of figures/tables, so I mark down one point for minor presentation uncertainty in those areas.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and flows logically from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are clearly stated early (GBS, coverage vectors/filters, subword handling). Notation appears consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, with thorough related work and reported hyperparameters; figures/tables/examples are effective in illustrating GBS. A few presentation gaps remain: the description of coverage vectors/filters and the runtime complexity claim would benefit from a slightly more formal specification and clearer illustrations of the grid-beam mechanics for the reader. Overall the exposition is strong with only minor clarity improvements needed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and states its main contributions early (GBS description, support for multi-token/discontinuous constraints, subword handling). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, and experimental details (datasets, baselines, hyperparameters) are provided, which aids reproducibility. The summary does not explicitly confirm clarity of notation or the effectiveness of figures/tables, so I mark down one point for minor presentation uncertainty in those areas.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a concrete, model-agnostic algorithm (Grid Beam Search) that meaningfully extends standard beam search to enforce multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints and to operate without retraining—a clear methodological innovation beyond vanilla time-based beam search. The framing of beams over timesteps and constraint-coverage dimensions and the use of coverage vectors/filters to manage constraint placement are non-trivial extensions that would inform practitioners; however, the problem of constrained decoding itself is well-studied and the paper admits no formal guarantees and a known runtime blow-up, which prevents rating it as a highly groundbreaking advance. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful extension to standard beam search—Grid Beam Search—that is model-agnostic and explicitly designed to enforce arbitrary multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints during decoding without retraining. Organizing beams over timesteps and constraint coverage and using coverage vectors/filters is a non-trivial methodological advance beyond vanilla beam search and constrained decoding heuristics, and the demonstrated applicability to subword constraints and interactive/domain-adaptation workflows would be informative to practitioners. While the problem of constrained decoding has been studied, the proposed grid formulation and its practical, training-free integration represent a clear and differentiated contribution rather than a mere incremental combination of existing techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a concrete, model-agnostic algorithm (Grid Beam Search) that meaningfully extends standard beam search to enforce multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints and to operate without retraining—a clear methodological innovation beyond vanilla time-based beam search. The framing of beams over timesteps and constraint-coverage dimensions and the use of coverage vectors/filters to manage constraint placement are non-trivial extensions that would inform practitioners; however, the problem of constrained decoding itself is well-studied and the paper admits no formal guarantees and a known runtime blow-up, which prevents rating it as a highly groundbreaking advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful extension to standard beam search—Grid Beam Search—that is model-agnostic and explicitly designed to enforce arbitrary multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints during decoding without retraining. Organizing beams over timesteps and constraint coverage and using coverage vectors/filters is a non-trivial methodological advance beyond vanilla beam search and constrained decoding heuristics, and the demonstrated applicability to subword constraints and interactive/domain-adaptation workflows would be informative to practitioners. While the problem of constrained decoding has been studied, the proposed grid formulation and its practical, training-free integration represent a clear and differentiated contribution rather than a mere incremental combination of existing techniques.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers solid, practically relevant empirical results across multiple datasets and addresses implementation details like subword handling and runtime tradeoffs. However, they also concur that the work lacks deeper analyses—formal guarantees, thorough ablations/sensitivity studies, and clearer reproducibility (code release)—which limits full understanding and adoption. Given the strong empirical contribution but missing analytical and reproducibility elements, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a reasonably diverse empirical evaluation (multiple corpora, BLEU gains in interactive post-editing and domain adaptation) and reports hyperparameters and known runtime cost O(ktc), but it lacks crucial analyses a Theory Purist requires: no formal specification or proofs of soundness/completeness for multi-token or discontinuous constraint satisfaction and no rigorous asymptotic or empirical runtime scaling across beam size and number of constraints beyond the stated naive bound. Ablation studies isolating the contributions of coverage vectors, filters, and subword handling are not reported in the summary, limiting attribution of gains. Related work is thorough and some limitations are discussed, but reproducibility is only partial (code not mentioned).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid empirical evidence across multiple realistic datasets (Europarl, CommonCrawl, JRC-Aquis, OpenSubtitles, Autodesk post-editing) showing BLEU gains for interactive post-editing and domain adaptation, and addresses practical concerns (subword handling of rare tokens, parallelizability to mitigate decoding cost). It also explicitly discusses runtime complexity and beam-size tradeoffs and reports hyperparameters. Missing, from the summary, are detailed ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., systematic beam/constraint scaling experiments) and clarity on code release/reproducibility, which are important for adoptability in production settings—hence a score of 4.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, well-motivated problem in sequence generation, is model-agnostic, evaluated on realistic datasets, and is a good fit for the target NLP/ML audience. Reviewer A’s concerns about formal guarantees and more detailed complexity discussion are valid but appear to be minor scope issues that do not undermine the practical contribution or empirical validation. Given the strong applicability, sound experimental evaluation, and deployment relevance, a top-tier fit is justified.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a canonical problem in NLP sequence generation (lexically constrained decoding) and presents an algorithm (Grid Beam Search) that is immediately relevant to top NLP/ML venues, evaluated on standard MT corpora and interactive post-editing scenarios. It uses expected methods and metrics (attention-based NMT, beam search baselines, BLEU) and targets the right audience. I have minor scope concerns: the work relies on an engineering-style extension without formal guarantees about soundness/completeness for multi-token/discontinuous constraints or rigorous worst-case complexity bounds beyond the stated O(ktc) growth, which top-tier reviewers in this community will expect to be addressed or discussed in depth.

reviewer_b.justification: This is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: the paper addresses a practically important sequence-generation problem (lexical constraints) with a model-agnostic method that requires no retraining, which aligns with production-focused research I value. Experiments use realistic datasets (Europarl, CommonCrawl, JRC-Aquis, OpenSubtitles, Autodesk post-editing) and demonstrate tangible gains for interactive post-editing and domain adaptation, matching community evaluation norms (BLEU) and deployment concerns. The runtime limitations are acknowledged and mitigated (parallelization, subword handling for OOVs), and the work is positioned squarely for the machine-translation and sequence-generation audience at the conference.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental setup is fair and evaluations (BLEU, practical scenarios, runtime discussion) are well reported, but they also agree that the paper omits direct comparisons to other constrained-decoding algorithms. Because this omission materially weakens the empirical claims about the algorithmic contribution, I rate the meaningfulness of the comparisons as moderate rather than strong, reflecting the paper’s solid reporting but incomplete set of relevant baselines.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares GBS to standard time-based beam search and baseline attention-based NMT models on multiple datasets with BLEU, which is a fair and consistent experimental setup. However, it does not appear to compare against other algorithmic constrained-decoding methods or strong algorithmic baselines that directly address lexical constraints, which is a significant omission given the paper's algorithmic contribution; this weakens claims of empirical superiority. The authors do analyze runtime trade-offs (noting O(ktc) vs O(kt)) and discuss related work thoroughly, but the lack of direct comparisons to competing constrained decoding algorithms prevents a fully convincing evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares GBS to appropriate baseline NMT beam search models on the stated corpora using BLEU and evaluates practical scenarios (post-editing, domain adaptation), and it documents limitations (runtime, rare tokens). Related work coverage is described as thorough and differences are analyzed, but empirical baselines appear limited to standard time-based beam search rather than other prior constrained-decoding algorithms or stronger contemporary constrained-generation baselines. From an applied-systems perspective this is a minor omission but comparisons are otherwise fair and well-reported.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a coherent, practically effective algorithm and substantiates its main claims with empirical gains while acknowledging runtime costs. However, they raise legitimate concerns about the lack of formal guarantees for multi-token/discontinuous constraints, limited ablations/statistical testing, and some reproducibility details. Given the clear algorithmic correctness at an implementation level and solid empirical evidence, but with noted gaps in theoretical and experimental thoroughness, a score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with empirical experiments showing BLEU gains in post-editing and domain adaptation scenarios, and it correctly acknowledges runtime costs (GBS: O(k t c) vs standard O(k t)). However, from the provided summary there are no formal proofs or rigorous correctness guarantees for soundness/completeness of multi-token or discontinuous constraint satisfaction (coverage vectors and filters are described but not formally specified), and the runtime/complexity discussion is limited to a high-level note rather than tight bounds or empirical scaling analyses. Additionally the evaluation appears to lack crucial ablations that isolate the effect of components (coverage vectors, filters, subword handling) and no mention is made of statistical testing, so some claims about effectiveness and trade-offs remain insufficiently supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states and demonstrates the main claim that Grid Beam Search (GBS) enables lexically constrained decoding without retraining — experiments across multiple corpora (Europarl, CommonCrawl, JRC-Aquis, OpenSubtitles, Autodesk post-editing) show sizable BLEU gains and practical use in Pick-Revise workflows. The algorithmic description (coverage vectors, start/continue constraint handling) is coherent and matches the implementation-level claims, and the paper acknowledges and analyzes the added O(ktc) runtime cost and subword-based mitigation for unseen tokens. Minor concerns remain about thoroughness of experimental controls (statistical significance, availability of code/reproducibility) and the dependence of effectiveness on beam size, but these do not undermine the core technical validity.

main_claims: Grid Beam Search (GBS) extends beam search to allow inclusion of pre-specified lexical constraints (words or phrases) during sequence decoding without modifying model parameters or training data.
GBS supports multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints and uses coverage vectors and filters to ensure correct placement and non-repetition of constraints.
Using subword units with GBS handles unknown or rare tokens in user-specified constraints, enabling domain adaptation scenarios.

GBS yields large improvements in translation quality in interactive post-editing scenarios (e.g., Pick-Revise) and can provide significant gains for domain adaptation, while being parallelizable to mitigate added computational cost.
_summary.methodology: Grid Beam Search organizes beams in a grid over timesteps and constraint coverage, allowing hypotheses to generate, start, or continue constraints; it operates on top of any sequence model (here NMT with attention) and maintains coverage vectors and filters to enforce multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints.
limitations: Naive GBS runtime complexity is O(ktc), multiplying beams by number of constraints, increasing computational cost compared to standard beam search O(kt).
Risk that user-specified constraints contain tokens unseen in training data (mitigated by using subword units).
Performance and speed depend on beam size; keeping beam size small is necessary for efficiency.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - neural machine translation / sequence generation

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in theoretical machine learning and formal methods with a history of work on provable properties of sequence models and decoding algorithms.
traits: Penalizes papers that present algorithmic modifications without formal guarantees about correctness or complexity (e.g., no worst-case time/space bounds)
Requires formal specification or proofs for claims about constraint satisfaction (e.g., soundness/complete coverage of multi-token/discontinuous constraints)

Subtracts points if empirical evaluation lacks comprehensive ablations that isolate contributions (e.g., effect of coverage vectors, filters, and subword treatment) or if comparisons to strong algorithmic baselines are missing
Demands measured and analytically justified trade-offs for added computation (e.g., asymptotic complexity and empirical runtime scaling across beam size and number of constraints)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on formal properties and algorithmic novelty; expects rigorous proofs or theoretical analyses to back empirical claims and penalizes primarily engineering/empirical-only contributions.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Industry-experienced NLP researcher focused on deployable sequence generation systems, interactive MT workflows, and domain adaptation for production translation services.
traits: Rewards methods that are model-agnostic and simple to integrate into existing pipelines (gives positive credit for approaches that require no retraining or model changes)
Gives extra credit for evaluations that demonstrate practical utility in realistic scenarios (e.g., interactive post-editing, Pick-Revise workflows, domain adaptation with user-provided constraints)
Values empirical evidence of effectiveness and scalability (e.g., sizable BLEU/TER gains in applied settings and demonstration of parallelizability to mitigate decoding cost)
Prefers methods that handle real-world issues like rare/unknown tokens (e.g., use of subword units for constrained decoding) and will penalize if these aspects are not empirically validated
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and application-driven: prioritizes empirical impact, ease of adoption, and demonstrated utility in realistic workflows over formal analysis.
